Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit unconventional because I work part-time and have limited resources, so I had to be strategic. I'd usually carve out chunks of time on weekends and a couple of evenings during the week, aiming for about 20 hours total. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of that time creating diagrams, mind maps, and color-coded notes from the lecture materials. The gamified tasks actually helped me structure things better because earning those badges gave me small goals to work toward each week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what shifts I was working. I tried to keep some consistency, but real life doesn't always cooperate when you're juggling everything on a tight budget. Deadlines would make me compress a lot of studying into shorter windows, which ramped up my anxiety even though I managed to stay resilient through it. I found that having those 5 online courses simultaneously meant I was constantly reshuffling priorities.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline approaching or getting a notification about new content being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed since my attendance wasn't always great. The gamified elements also pulled me in occasionally because I wanted to see my progress on the leaderboard or complete a challenge.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became more habitual, especially when the gamified tasks were in full swing. I'd find myself logging in to see if I'd earned new points or if my ranking had changed. But honestly, it never became a deeply ingrained routine for me because my schedule was too unpredictable. The habit formed more around specific course milestones than daily use.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me since I learn best visually. I watched them multiple times, pausing to sketch out concepts and create my own visual summaries. The gamified tasks were also great because they broke down the material into manageable chunks and made completing them feel rewarding rather than just obligatory. When I could see my progress visually tracked, it motivated me to keep going.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums, only posting twice the whole term. It's not that I didn't value other perspectives, but with my anxiety, putting my thoughts out there publicly felt uncomfortable and time-consuming. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because I didn't have money for extra textbooks and the library copies were always checked out. I prioritized what directly impacted my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam, or if it had that gamified element that made progress tangible. I needed to see clear value, like points earned or a skill unlocked, because my time is limited and precious. Visual content that I could engage with actively rather than just passively read also felt more worthwhile to me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford to indulge in given my schedule and low motivation levels. Required ones had urgency built in, so I prioritized those automatically. With optional stuff, I'd think about whether it would actually help my grade or understanding, and if the answer was uncertain, I'd usually skip it. The gamified optional tasks were different though because they made even non-required work feel like it had stakes.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and videos when I had good wifi access, then study from my phone or laptop without being connected. I created physical flashcards and hand-drawn diagrams that I'd review on my commute to work. My notes were extensive and I'd study from those way more than from the actual LMS.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper for hours, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also discuss concepts with a friend from class over text or when we ran into each other, which was genuine learning but completely invisible. My visual study materials, like the mind maps I'd tape to my wall, represented serious engagement that no system could track.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it if possible, and then rewatch it offline multiple times while taking detailed notes. The initial access was just the starting point of my engagement with that material. I'd return to downloaded PDFs and my own notes repeatedly over weeks without ever logging back in to access them again.
Q12: Around midterms, my anxiety spiked significantly even though my stress levels were generally manageable. I was working extra shifts to cover some unexpected expenses, and my attendance dropped because I physically couldn't make it to class. The combination of financial pressure and academic demands made it really hard to engage consistently, even though I knew I needed to push through.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by having five courses at once, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS just to stay afloat. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules temporarily because tackling them required energy I didn't always have. My competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly, and sometimes the LMS activities lost out to more urgent life demands.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about money or just mentally drained. I'd technically be logged in and accumulating watch time, but I wasn't actually absorbing anything meaningful. Those sessions felt hollow, and I'd have to go back later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because my anxiety made public participation feel daunting. Even knowing they mattered for participation, I'd find excuses to delay until I'd missed the window for meaningful contribution. I also avoided some of the more challenging problem sets until the last minute because I was scared of confirming that I didn't understand the material as well as I hoped.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, watching videos I'd put off and completing tasks in concentrated bursts. Without deadlines, I might go days without logging in because other life demands would take over. The structure they provided was essential for someone with my low motivation levels.
Q17: When I got positive feedback on assignments, it genuinely boosted my mood and made me more likely to engage with the next module enthusiastically. The gamified feedback, like earning badges or seeing my score improve, had a similar effect. Negative or critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me hesitant to dive back into that topic immediately, though I'd eventually push through.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and the gamified elements really helped me know what to tackle and when. Having visual progress indicators aligned well with how I learn and process information. However, the structure also assumed consistent attendance which didn't match my reality, so I sometimes felt like I was playing catch-up rather than moving smoothly through the material.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement much easier because they turned work into something that felt achievable and rewarding. The visual progress tracking was perfect for someone like me who needs to see advancement concretely. What made it harder was when resources required being online constantly or when peer interaction was heavily weighted, since my schedule and anxiety made those challenging.
Q20: I think those students might be like me on my anxious days, logging in repeatedly to check things, watching videos without really processing them, or just feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start on actual submissions. High activity could also mean someone is struggling to understand the material and keeps revisiting it without making progress. Sometimes presence doesn't equal productive engagement.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I did a lot of my real learning offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes, so my LMS activity was lower than my actual engagement. My exam scores and assignment quality show that I understood the material even if I wasn't constantly logged in. Efficient studying doesn't always mean visible studying.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when someone logs in, what they click on, and deadline-driven spikes in activity. But they completely miss the depth of offline engagement, the quality of understanding, and the emotional experience of learning. They can't capture my anxiety while studying or the satisfaction when a concept finally clicks. Numbers tell a story, but it's not the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. For me, it's when I'm actively creating visual representations of concepts, asking myself questions, and connecting new information to what I already know. It's a mental state of curiosity and investment, which doesn't always correlate with how often I click on things in an LMS.
Q24: My extensive offline study routine, the visual learning materials I created, and the genuine intellectual engagement I had with the content wouldn't show up at all. My anxiety and how I managed it, my financial constraints affecting when and how I could study, and the resilience I built through the term are all invisible. The gamified tasks captured some of my engagement, but the full picture of who I am as a learner goes way beyond what any log could show.
